args_redacted.query: zero trust architecture NIST principles never trust always verify
defense in depth layered security strategy explained

args_redacted.raw:
{"path": "workspace/security_comparison.txt", "content": "================================================================================
  COMPARISON: ZERO TRUST SECURITY vs. DEFENSE IN DEPTH
================================================================================

1. OVERVIEW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST SECURITY
    Zero Trust is a security model built on the principle of "never trust,
    always verify." It assumes that threats can exist both outside and inside
    the network perimeter, and therefore no user, device, or application
    should be trusted by default — even if they are already inside the
    network. Every access request must be authenticated, authorized, and
    continuously validated before granting access to resources.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH
    Defense in Depth is a layered security strategy that employs multiple,
    overlapping defensive controls to protect information assets. The idea
    is that if one layer fails, the next layer will catch the threat. It
    draws inspiration from military strategy, where multiple lines of
    defense slow down and ultimately defeat an attacker.


2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES:
    • Never Trust, Always Verify — No implicit trust based on network location.
    • Least Privilege Access — Users and systems get only the minimum access
      needed to perform their function, and only for the duration needed.
    • Micro-Segmentation — Networks are divided into small, isolated zones;
      moving between zones requires separate authentication.
    • Continuous Monitoring & Validation — Sessions are constantly inspected
      and re-validated; trust is never permanent.
    • Assume Breach — Operate as if the network is already compromised;
      design systems to contain and limit blast radius.
    • Explicit Verification — Authenticate and authorize based on all available
      data points (identity, device health, location, service, data
      classification, anomalies).

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
    • Layered Controls — Deploy multiple security controls at different
      levels: physical, network, host, application, and data.
    • No Single Point of Failure — Redundancy ensures that the failure of
      one control does not result in complete compromise.
    • Diversity of Defenses — Use different types of controls (preventive,
      detective, corrective, deterrent) from different vendors to avoid
      monoculture risk.
    • Perimeter-First Mindset — Traditionally emphasizes a strong outer
      boundary (firewalls, IDS/IPS) with additional internal layers.
    • Defense at Every Level — Protect the physical premises, the network
      perimeter, internal network segments, individual hosts, applications,
      and the data itself.


3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| ASPECT                      | ZERO TRUST                      | DEFENSE IN DEPTH                |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Core Philosophy             | Trust is a vulnerability;       | Layers compensate for the       |
|                             | eliminate implicit trust        | weaknesses of individual        |
|                             | entirely.                       | controls.                       |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Network Assumption          | The network is always hostile;  | The perimeter can be fortified; |
|                             | internal vs. external is        | internal traffic is somewhat    |
|                             | irrelevant.                     | more trusted than external.     |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Primary Focus               | Identity-centric: who are you,  | Infrastructure-centric: where   |
|                             | what device, what context?      | are you, what layers protect    |
|                             |                                  | this asset?                     |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Access Control              | Dynamic, risk-based, per-       | Often static, role-based,       |
|                             | session, continuously re-       | granted at perimeter or         |
|                             | evaluated.                      | segment boundary.               |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Lateral Movement            | Designed to prevent lateral     | Can slow lateral movement but   |
|                             | movement via micro-segmentation | may not fully prevent it if     |
|                             | and per-request auth.           | perimeter is breached.          |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Implementation Complexity   | High — requires identity        | Moderate — builds on existing   |
|                             | federation, device trust,       | layered controls; familiar to   |
|                             | policy engines, and significant | most security teams.            |
|                             | architectural changes.          |                                  |
+

    - Diversity of Defenses: Use different types of controls (preventive,

    - Perimeter-First Mindset: Traditionally emphasizes a strong outer
      boundary (firewalls, IDS/IPS) with additional internal layers.
    - Defense at Every Level: Protect the physical premises, the network
      perimeter, internal network segments, individual hosts, applications,
      and the data itself.


3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ASPECT                  | ZERO TRUST                         | DEFENSE IN DEPTH
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Core Philosophy         | Trust is a vulnerability;          | Layers compensate for the
                        | eliminate implicit trust entirely. | weaknesses of individual controls.
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Network Assumption      | The network is always hostile;     | The perimeter can be fortified;
                        | internal vs. external is           | internal traffic is somewhat
                        | irrelevant.                        | more trusted than external.
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Primary Focus           | Identity-centric: who are you,     | Infrastructure-centric: where
                        | what device, what context?         | are you, what layers protect
                        |                                     | this asset?
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Access Control          | Dynamic, risk-based, per-session,  | Often static, role-based,
                        | continuously re-evaluated.         | granted at perimeter or
                        |                                     | segment boundary.
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Lateral Movement        | Designed to prevent lateral        | Can slow lateral movement but
                        | movement via micro-segmentation    | may not fully prevent it if
                        | and per-request authentication.    | perimeter is breached.
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Implementation          | High: requires identity            | Moderate: builds on existing
Complexity              | federation, device trust, policy   | layered controls; familiar to
                        | engines, architectural changes.    | most security teams.
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Maturity                | Relatively newer; still evolving   | Well-established; decades of
                        | (formalized by Forrester in 2010,  | proven practice in military
                        | NIST SP 800-207 published 2020).   | and enterprise environments.
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Typical Technologies    | MFA, IAM, CASB, ZTNA, SDP,         | Firewalls, IDS/IPS, antivirus,
                        | micro-segmentation, endpoint       | EDR, SIEM, DLP, encryption,
                        | detection, continuous auth.        | physical security, VLANs, NAC.
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

{"content": "================================================================================
  COMPARISON: ZERO TRUST SECURITY vs. DEFENSE IN DEPTH
================================================================================

1. OVERVIEW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST SECURITY
    Zero Trust is a modern security model built on the principle of "never
    trust, always verify." It assumes that threats can exist both outside
    and inside the network perimeter, and therefore no user, device, or
    application should be trusted by default — even if already inside the
    network. Every access request must be authenticated, authorized, and
    continuously validated before granting access to resources.

    The term was coined by Forrester Research in 2010 and later formalized
    by NIST in SP 800-207 (2020). It has gained widespread adoption with
    the rise of cloud computing, remote work, and increasingly sophisticated
    threats that render the traditional perimeter model obsolete.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH
    Defense in Depth is a layered security strategy that employs multiple,
    overlapping defensive controls to protect information assets. The idea
    is that if one layer fails, the next layer will catch the threat. It
    draws inspiration from military strategy, where multiple lines of
    defense slow down and ultimately defeat an attacker.

    This approach has been a cornerstone of cybersecurity for decades and
    is widely recognized in frameworks such as ISO 27001, NIST CSF, and
    CIS Controls. It remains a foundational strategy for most organizations.


2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES:
    • Never Trust, Always Verify — No implicit trust based on network
      location. Every access request is treated as if it originates from
      an untrusted network.

    • Least Privilege Access — Users and systems receive only the minimum
      permissions needed to perform their function, and only for the
      duration required (just-in-time access).

    • Micro-Segmentation — Networks are divided into small, isolated zones.
      Moving between zones requires separate authentication and authorization,
      dramatically limiting lateral movement.

    • Continuous Monitoring & Validation — Sessions are constantly inspected
      and re-validated. Trust is never permanent; it is continuously assessed
      based on behavior, device posture, and context.

    • Assume Breach — Operate as if the network is already compromised.
      Design systems to contain and limit the blast radius of any breach.
      This mindset drives architectural decisions.

    • Explicit Verification — Authenticate and authorize based on all
      available data points: user identity, device health, location, time
      of day, service being accessed, data classification, and behavioral
      anomalies.

    • Dynamic Policy — Access policies adapt in real time based on risk
      scoring. A request that is low-risk in one context may be high-risk
      in another.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
    • Layered Controls — Deploy multiple security controls at different
      levels: physical, network, host, application, and data. Each layer
      provides a distinct type of protection.

    • No Single Point of Failure — Redundancy ensures that the failure of
      one control does not result in complete compromise. If the firewall
      misses something, the IDS/IPS may catch it; if that fails, endpoint
      protection may stop it.

    • Diversity of Defenses — Use different types of controls (preventive,
      detective, corrective, deterrent) and ideally from different vendors
      to avoid monoculture risk where a single vulnerability defeats all
      layers.

    • Perimeter-First Mindset — Traditionally emphasizes a strong outer
      boundary (firewalls, IDS/IPS) with additional internal layers. The
      perimeter is the first and most critical line of defense.

    • Defense at Every Level — Protect the physical premises (guards, locks,
      cameras), the network perimeter (firewalls, DMZs), internal network
      segments (VLANs, NAC), individual hosts (antivirus, EDR, hardening),
      applications (WAF, secure coding), and the data itself (encryption,
      DLP, backups).

    • Layered Depth Within Each Layer — Even within a single layer, multiple
      controls may exist. For example, at the network layer: firewalls,
      IDS/IPS, network segmentation, and traffic analysis all work together.


3. DETAILED COMPARISON TABLE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ASPECT                  | ZERO TRUST                         | DEFENSE IN DEPTH
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Core Philosophy         | Trust is a vulnerability;          | Layers compensate for the
